summary: A rare spring cup fungus of old spruce forests whose dark cauldron-like fruit bodies are unforgettable, but whose GBIF map still reflects targeted searching and dataset mobilization as much as biology.

fieldRecord.habitatNote: The linked photos show a mature fruit body seated directly in moss and conifer litter, matching the species' typical spring appearance on the forest floor in spruce-dominated habitat.
fieldRecord.observationNote: EXIF-backed photo record from the linked image set. Running scripts/build_site_data.py will fill date, time and coordinates from the matching photos when metadata are present.
fieldRecord.locationLabel: Ekensberg, Uppsala kommun, Uppsala län, Sverige

media.caption: Close view of the dark fluid-filled cup and wrinkled outer wall that make Witches Cauldron unlike almost any other spring fungus.
Side view of a mature Sarcosoma globosum showing the thick folded wall and black inner disc set low in moss and needle litter.
Wider habitat view showing how easily a fruit body disappears into the spring forest floor despite its unusual shape.

hero.lede: Witches Cauldron is the kind of fungus people remember after one encounter: a dark, thick-walled cup sitting low in moss and needle litter like a small vessel of black water. It fruits shortly after snowmelt, can vanish again before most people start looking for fungi, and is strongly tied to old forest structure, which makes it an excellent revisit species for early spring walks in spruce country.
hero.shortMorphology: Dark brown to black, gelatinous and urn- to cauldron-shaped fruit body with a smooth black inner disc and a thick wrinkled outer wall, usually emerging low in moss and conifer litter.

taxonomy.source: GBIF species match with Catalogue of Life checklistKey
taxonomy.resolvedAgainstDataset: Catalogue of Life checklist via GBIF species/match?checklistKey=7ddf754f-d193-4cc9-b351-99906754a03b

distribution.globalSummary: IUCN treats Sarcosoma globosum as a circumpolar boreal to hemiboreal fungus with its main strongholds in northern Europe, especially Sweden, Finland and parts of Russia. The filtered GBIF layer used here adds 2,010 post-2000 human or machine observations, but that footprint mixes targeted fungal searches, mobilized legacy data and some eastern North American reports that the IUCN assessment says may include misidentified look-alikes, so it should be read as an observation surface rather than a clean native-range map.
distribution.swedenSummary: Sweden has only 34 filtered GBIF observations under the project map settings, but the national conservation importance is much larger than that small open-data count suggests. The 2025 Swedish Red List classifies the species as EN, and the local red-list documentation explicitly notes that Sweden holds a large share of the global population.

notes: The map shows aggregated GBIF occurrence density rather than individual points.
Filters are restricted to HUMAN_OBSERVATION and MACHINE_OBSERVATION records from year 2000 onwards and to records with occurrenceStatus = PRESENT.
The webpage renders this layer as a hex summary map.

distribution.samplingBiasNote: GBIF hex density here reflects who is searching for rare fungi, which historical records have been digitized, and where specialist fungal observation culture is strongest. The recent Fungal Ecology analysis of this species specifically shows that rising observation totals can reflect observer culture and reporting effort, so dense or sparse hexes should not be treated as direct abundance.

internationalContext.summary: Internationally, Sarcosoma globosum is a rare old-forest spring ascomycete with a published global IUCN status of Near Threatened from 2015. Its ecology is informative but not fully settled: recent studies describe the habitat well, yet the IUCN assessment still notes uncertainty over whether the species is strictly saprotrophic, mycorrhizal, or something more complicated.
ecology: Fruit bodies appear shortly after snowmelt in spring and can persist for weeks, so detectability depends strongly on timing.
Repeated sources place the species in older spruce-dominated forests with moss and conifer litter, often on nutrient-rich sandy or alluvial soils near brooks, ravines, eskers or moraine ridges.
The mycelium is thought to be long-lived and known sites may fruit sporadically rather than every year, which helps explain why one failed search is not strong evidence of absence.

Recent habitat work from Lithuania found all known sites in coniferous forests with Picea abies, mostly in older stands, though some sites persisted in culturally influenced spruce forests or plantations.
The trophic mode remains uncertain: some recent papers describe the fungus as saprotrophic, but the IUCN assessment still says it is unclear whether the species is saprotrophic or mycorrhizal.
threats: Clear-cutting and broader logging-related habitat degradation remove the old spruce structure this species depends on, and the IUCN assessment notes that it does not seem to re-colonize the managed forests that replace original habitat.
Abandonment of historical forest grazing in parts of Fennoscandia has allowed denser forest structure that appears less suitable than the older semi-open conditions once maintained at some sites.
Local disturbance from roads, building, hydrological change and spruce decline can degrade sites faster than the species can shift or recover.

phenology.northernEurope: Fruiting usually starts soon after snowmelt in early spring; the exact window shifts with weather and can close before the main autumn mushroom season begins.
phenology.note: This is a strong revisit species for early spring forest walks rather than autumn forays.

swedishContext.summary: In Sweden, bombmurkla is not just a curiosity but a conservation-responsibility species. Artfakta/Dyntaxa accepts Sarcosoma globosum and recommends bombmurkla as the Swedish name, while the local 2025 Red List workbook classifies it as Starkt hotad (EN) under criterion A3c. The Swedish documentation places it in older bilberry and meadow-spruce forests on sandy mixed soils, often near moraine ridges and brook depressions, and notes that Sweden holds a large share of the global population while bark beetle-driven spruce damage is worsening several sites.

redList2025.documentation: Artens levnadssätt är okänt. Den bildar fruktkroppar i mossa och barrförna på sandblandad mark i äldre blåbärs- och ängsgranskog, ofta vid moränryggar och i närheten av bäckdråg. Arten är välkänd och ofta eftersökt, med många nu försvunna lokaler. De senaste årens granbarkborreangrepp har försämrat statusen på flera av artens växtplatser i kärnområdet. Med förväntat ökade skogsskador i Götaland och Svealand bedöms hotnivån stiga. Sverige hyser en stor del av artens globala population.

keyFacts: Artfakta/Dyntaxa taxon 1445 treats Sarcosoma globosum as accepted and bombmurkla as the recommended Swedish name.
The Swedish Red List 2025 cache classifies the species as EN under criterion A3c.
The local workbook treats the species as bofast och reproducerande, with immigration history spontan.
The Swedish red-list documentation notes many historical localities are gone and that recent spruce bark beetle outbreaks have worsened habitat quality in parts of the core area.
Because fruiting is early and irregular, revisiting known habitat soon after snowmelt is more useful than waiting for the main autumn fungus season.

artfakta.note: Use Artfakta as the main Swedish species-information source when refreshing this page locally.

literature.title: Habitat Diversity, Environmental Conditions, and Distribution of Endangered Fungus Sarcosoma globosum (Ascomycota) in Lithuania
literature.whyRelevant: Best recent DOI-backed habitat paper here for stand structure, spruce association, local disturbance and the edge-of-range situation in northeastern Europe.

literature.title: Increase in Sarcosoma globosum observations reveals new fungal observation culture
literature.whyRelevant: Directly useful for the GBIF bias discussion because it shows that rising record totals can reflect observation culture and search effort rather than a simple biological increase.

literature.title: Sarcosoma globosum - an indicator of climate change?
literature.whyRelevant: Useful for spring phenology, Finnish habitat context and the question of whether changing detection patterns and climate cues might shift where the species is noticed.

literature.title: On the biology and ecology of Sarcosoma globosum in the middle taiga belt of West Siberia
literature.journal: Environmental Dynamics and Global Climate Change
literature.whyRelevant: Adds direct field ecology on fruiting frequency, spring timing and habitat conditions from a Siberian part of the range.
